(Qualtek QAWA-30-5) through the PJ-037AH DC barrel jack.

T~L~35~-460~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~C12 (100 µF) provides bulk filtering and helps stabilize
the 5V input rail during load transients.
 
C11 (100 nF) provides high-frequency decoupling for the
input supply and reduces switching and line noise.~1~start~gge15915~0~pinpart

system and powers the Raspberry Pi, Raspberry Pi Pico,
and the PCB 5V loads.~1~start~gge15957~0~pinpart

T~L~45~-750~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~PICO INTERFACE CONTROLLER
 
• Pico receives push-button states from MCP I/O expander via I2C (SDA, SCL).
 
• Button events are transmitted to the main Raspberry Pi using UART
  communication (GPIO21 RX, GPIO22 TX).
 
• Pico controls LED driver devices through serial control interface
  (SIN, SCLK, XLAT, BLANK).
 
• Pico is powered from the system 5V rail applied to VSYS (Pin 39)
  from the PCB power input.
 
• Pico onboard regulator generates 3.3V which powers the MCP device,
  TLC logic supply, and I2C pull-up resistors.
 
• Pico acts only as a communication and control interface between
  the front panel hardware and the main Raspberry Pi system.~1~start~gge28995~0~pinpart

T~L~55~-270~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~0 Ω resistors are placed in series with the 5V supply to Raspberry Pi Pico.
 
When these resistors are installed, the Raspberry Pi Pico is powered
from the PCB 5V power rail.
 
If the resistors are removed, the Raspberry Pi Pico can instead be
powered through its USB power connector.~1~start~gge31100~0~pinpart
T~L~55~-150~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~Power supply filtering:\n0.1 µF capacitor for high-frequency decoupling.\n100 µF bulk capacitor for 5V rail stabilization.~1~start~gge32765~0~pinpart

T~L~60~-740~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~RASPBERRY PI – PICO COMMUNICATION NOTE
 
• Raspberry Pi is powered from the PCB 5V supply through
  GPIO pins 2 and 4.
 
• UART communication between Raspberry Pi and Pico uses
  GPIO14 (TXD, Pin 8) and GPIO15 (RXD, Pin 10).
 
• Pico UART pins are:
  Pin 21 → TX
  Pin 22 → RX
 
• UART signals must be crossed:
  Pi TX (GPIO14) → Pico RX (Pin 22)
  Pi RX (GPIO15) → Pico TX (Pin 21)
 
• Raspberry Pi communicates with the Tektronix oscilloscope
  through the USB interface.
 
• When programming through the USB-C port, external PCB
  power should be turned OFF to avoid powering the Raspberry
  Pi from two sources simultaneously.
 
• PCB power can be disabled using the system power switch
  or by removing the GPIO header jumper connection.~1~start~gge2495~0~pinpart

T~L~680~-725~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~0 Ω resistors are placed in series with the 5V supply to Raspberry Pi
GPIO pins 2 and 4.
 
When these resistors are installed, the Raspberry Pi is powered
from the PCB 5V power rail.
 
If the resistors are removed, the Raspberry Pi can instead be
powered through its USB-C power connector.
 
These resistors provide a simple way to select the Raspberry Pi
power source during testing or future system changes.~1~start~gge4050~0~pinpart
T~L~670~-175~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~Power supply filtering:\n0.1 µF capacitor for high-frequency decoupling.\n100 µF bulk capacitor for 5V rail stabilization.~1~start~gge4242~0~pinpart

T~L~680~-560~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~Display used in the system:
4D Systems 4DHDMI-70-800-480-CT-EAR-500
 
7.0" HDMI TFT LCD display
Resolution: 800 x 480
Capacitive touch panel
Brightness: 500 cd/m²
Operating voltage: 4.8V – 5.2V
 
The display is connected externally to the Raspberry Pi
using standard HDMI and USB connections.
 
Video signal is provided through the Raspberry Pi HDMI port.
 
Touch interface is provided through the Raspberry Pi USB port.
 
The display is powered from the Raspberry Pi USB port
and therefore no dedicated display power circuitry is
implemented on this PCB.
 
Because the display is externally powered and connected
through standard cables, a separate schematic sheet for
the display interface is not required.
 
All display connections are handled directly by the
Raspberry Pi hardware interface.~1~start~gge4769~0~pinpart

T~L~30~-720~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~MCP23017 I/O expanders are powered from the Pico 3.3V rail and connected
to the shared I2C bus (SCL, SDA).
 
Each device provides 16 programmable GPIO pins arranged as
two 8-bit ports (GPIOA and GPIOB) used for reading push buttons
and rotary encoders on the front panel.
 
 
Operating voltage range: 1.8V – 5.5V
Typical supply current: ~1 mA per device
Maximum I2C clock frequency: up to 1.7 MHz.
 
 
 
Each device uses a unique hardware address through pins
A0, A1, and A2.
 
Address configuration used in this design:
 
MCP1 → A2=0 A1=0 A0=1 → I2C address 0x20
MCP2 → A2=0 A1=0 A0=0 → I2C address 0x21
MCP3 → A2=0 A1=1 A0=0 → I2C address 0x22
MCP4 → A2=0 A1=1 A0=1 → I2C address 0x23
 
 
RESET is pulled up to 3.3V through a 10 kΩ resistor.
 
 
INTA and INTB are not used in this design and are left
unconnected.
 
 
A 0.1 µF decoupling capacitor is placed across VDD and VSS
for each MCP23017 device.
 
 
Estimated total current consumption for four devices
is approximately 4 mA from the Pico 3.3V regulator.~1~start~gge18648~0~pinpart

T~L~700~-450~0~#0000FF~~~~~~comment~Three TLC5947 24-channel constant-current LED drivers are used
to control the front-panel LEDs.
 
The devices are daisy-chained through the serial data interface:
Pico SIN → TLC1 SIN
TLC1 SOUT → TLC2 SIN
TLC2 SOUT → TLC3 SIN
TLC3 SOUT is unused.
 
SCLK, XLAT, and BLANK are shared by all three devices and are
driven by the Raspberry Pi Pico.
 
Each TLC5947 is powered from the 3.3V logic rail.
The exposed thermal pad is connected to GND.
 
Each device includes a 0.1 µF decoupling capacitor between
VCC and GND.
 
BLANK is connected to the Pico and also pulled low through a
10 kΩ resistor to define a known startup state.
 
LED anodes are powered directly from the main +5V PCB supply.
The TLC5947 does not source LED power; each OUTx pin acts as a
constant-current sink output.
 
The output current of each channel is set by the RIREF resistor.
For RIREF = 3.3 kΩ:
 
IOUT ≈ 11.8 mA per channel
 
Maximum sink current per TLC5947 with all 24 channels on:
≈ 283 mA
 
Maximum sink current for three TLC5947 devices:
≈ 850 mA
 
Each TLC5947 provides 24 PWM-controlled output channels with
12-bit grayscale control (4096 levels).~1~start~gge9344~0~pinpart

T~L~110~-455~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~BLANK is controlled by the Raspberry Pi Pico and is used
to disable LED outputs during PWM refresh cycles.
 
A 10 kΩ pull-down resistor ensures the BLANK signal
remains LOW during startup to prevent floating inputs.~1~start~gge28810~0~pinpart
T~L~110~-385~0~#0000FF~~9pt~~~~comment~BLANK = HIGH  → LEDs OFF\nBLANK = LOW   → LEDs operate normally~1~start~gge28822~0~pinpart
